data.title: Elis Taps €600 Million Bond To Rebalance Debt And Fund Buybacks
data.description:
• Elis (ENXTPA:ELIS) has priced €600 million in senior unsecured notes under its EMTN programme.
  • The funds are intended for debt refinancing and a new share buyback program.
  • The placement was strongly oversubscribed, indicating solid demand from investors.

Elis, a provider of textile, hygiene and facility services, operates in sectors where clients often rely on long term outsourcing contracts, from hospitality and healthcare to industry. Recent years have kept attention on operational efficiency and service reliability across these areas, as customers balance cost control with hygiene and sustainability requirements. In this context, balance sheet flexibility and funding terms can matter almost as much as day to day operating performance.

The new €600 million notes give Elis an additional tool to shape its capital structure and share count. For investors, the mix of refinancing and buybacks affects both financial risk and how future earnings are spread across each share. How management sequences these uses of capital, and at what terms, will be key for anyone tracking ENXTPA:ELIS as the plan is executed.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Elis by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Elis.

Is Elis's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

For Elis, issuing €600 million of senior unsecured notes with a 6 year maturity and a 3.875% fixed coupon locks in medium term funding at a known cost while keeping assets unpledged. Around half of the proceeds are earmarked to refinance €300 million of notes due in May 2027, which helps smooth the debt maturity profile rather than letting a single year carry a large refinancing task. The remaining funds are intended for the 2026 share buyback program, so the balance sheet shift is not just about gross debt; it is also about how many shares future earnings are spread across. The order book above €2,000 million points to strong demand for Elis credit, but it also means investors should pay attention to total leverage and interest expense once both the new bond and the buyback are in place.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Elis Narrative

  • The transaction supports the existing narrative that cash generation and capital allocation, including buybacks, are an important part of Elis’s appeal for investors focused on recurring revenue and scale.
  • Using part of the proceeds for a share buyback after issuing fresh debt could challenge the narrative for readers who are cautious about higher leverage in a business that already requires ongoing capital expenditure.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on outsourcing momentum, sustainability and geographic expansion, while this new layer of fixed coupon debt and its impact on future interest costs may not be fully reflected in those storylines.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Elis to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher gross debt and interest commitments from the €600 million notes could weigh on financial flexibility if trading conditions worsen or if capital needs increase.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged that Elis has a high level of debt, so adding new senior unsecured notes makes it even more important to track leverage and refinancing options over the 6 year life of the bond.
  • 🎁 The oversubscribed order book, above €2,000 million for a €600 million deal, signals that bond investors see Elis’s business model as resilient enough to support this financing.
  • 🎁 Directing part of the proceeds to a 2026 share buyback program can reduce the share count, which, if earnings remain solid, may support earnings per share and dividends such as the proposed €0.48 per share cash dividend for the 2025 financial year.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how Elis’s net debt evolves as the 2027 notes are refinanced and the buyback program is executed, because that balance will shape debt to equity and interest coverage ratios. It is also worth tracking how management sequences future dividends, acquisitions and capital expenditure while servicing a 3.875% coupon, especially given the company’s ongoing need for investment in linen and facilities. Finally, watch for any updates on credit metrics or ratings, as these will influence the cost and terms of future debt issuance.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value.

For BeOne Medicines, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow stands at about $487.1 million. Analysts and model estimates then project Free Cash Flow out over the next decade, starting with $593.1 million in 2026 and reaching $4,673.2 million in 2035. When those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $789.94 per share. Compared with the current share price of $274.73, the DCF output suggests the stock is 65.2% undervalued on this method.

data.title: Gilead Expands Into Autoimmune With Ouro Deal And Galapagos Alliance
data.description:

  • The company is also entering a major research partnership with Galapagos to co-develop and commercialize this first-in-class clinical asset for severe autoimmune conditions.
  • These moves extend Gilead’s recent deal activity beyond oncology and broaden its inflammation and immunology portfolio.

For you as an investor, this places Gilead’s core antiviral and oncology focus alongside a deeper push into autoimmune disease therapies. The Ouro Medicines acquisition, together with the Galapagos partnership, adds a differentiated T cell engager candidate to the inflammation portfolio at a time when large drugmakers are seeking more specialized immune system targets.

Looking ahead, attention is likely to center on clinical progress for the fast-tracked, orphan-designated asset and on how Gilead structures development and commercialization with Galapagos. Deal terms, regulatory milestones, and any future label expansions for the T cell engager could all influence how material this move becomes in Gilead’s broader diversification efforts.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider American Express (AXP) After Recent Share Price Pullback?
data.description:
• Wondering whether American Express at around US$301.91 is still offering value, or if most of the upside is already reflected in the price.
  • The stock has posted a 1.2% return over the last 7 days, a 12.8% decline over 30 days, is down 19.0% year to date, yet shows a 9.8% return over 1 year, 91.9% over 3 years and 125.3% over 5 years. This raises fair questions about timing and risk.
  • Recent headlines have focused on American Express as a long term compounder in consumer finance, with attention on how its brand, rewards ecosystem and affluent customer base may support its position through different economic conditions. At the same time, commentators have highlighted competitive pressure from other payment networks and digital wallets, which helps explain some of the shorter term price swings you might be seeing.
  • Simply Wall St's valuation model gives American Express a 5 out of 6 valuation score. The rest of this article will walk through the key valuation approaches behind that number, before finishing with a broader way to think about what the stock is really worth over time.

American Express delivered 9.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Consumer Finance industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: American Express Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate above the return that equity investors typically require, then adds the value of those "excess" profits to its book value.

For American Express, the model starts with a Book Value of $48.80 per share and a Stable EPS of $21.04 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 13 analysts. data.title: Is Tutor Perini (TPC) Still Attractive After A 186% One Year Share Price Surge?
data.description:
• If you are trying to work out whether Tutor Perini at around US$72 a share still offers value, you need to look past the headline price and focus on what the current valuation actually reflects.
  • The stock has been volatile recently, with a 1.6% return over the last week, a 16.6% decline over the past month, a 4.5% gain year to date, and a very large 1 year return of 186.4%, alongside a long term gain of more than 10x over 3 years and 316.3% over 5 years.
  • Those moves have drawn fresh attention to Tutor Perini, especially as investors reassess construction and infrastructure names and what risks and opportunities are currently priced in. Recent coverage has focused on how the share price reacts to contract wins, project updates, and changing expectations around the company, which helps explain why the chart has been so active.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Tutor Perini scores a 4 out of 6 valuation score. The rest of this article will break that down across different valuation methods and will also point you to a more complete way to think about the stock's value at the end.

Tutor Perini delivered 186.4% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Construction industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Tutor Perini Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using an appropriate rate. It is essentially asking what the stream of expected future cash is worth in current dollars.

For Tutor Perini, the latest reported Free Cash Flow is about $638.7 million. The model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, with analyst inputs for the next few years and further projections extrapolated by Simply Wall St out to 2035. For example, projected Free Cash Flow is $500 million in 2026 and $607.8 million in 2035, all in $ terms.

Adding up those discounted cash flows produces an estimated intrinsic value of $163.77 per share. Compared with a current share price around $72, the DCF output suggests the stock is about 55.8% undervalued on this set of assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Tutor Perini is undervalued by 55.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 55 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Tutor Perini.

Approach 2: Tutor Perini Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it anchors the share price to actual earnings. You are essentially asking how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of profit.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on what the market expects for growth and how risky those earnings appear. Higher growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher uncertainty tend to justify a lower one.

Tutor Perini currently trades on a P/E of 47.54x. That is above the Construction industry average P/E of 33.18x and above the peer group average of 29.30x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio of 63.70x for Tutor Perini, which reflects factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the sector because it adjusts for the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming all Construction stocks deserve the same multiple. With Tutor Perini’s actual P/E of 47.54x below the Fair Ratio of 63.70x, this approach points to the shares trading below what that framework would suggest.

Result: UNDERVALUED
